title: Apple pie
subtitle: Double shortcrust casing a Bramley apple filling with cinnamon and brown sugar
excerpt: A 23 cm double-crust apple pie: a shortcrust base and lid enclosing a filling of Bramley apple slices with brown sugar, cinnamon, and lemon juice. The base is partially blind-baked to prevent a soggy bottom. Serves 6–8.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the apple pie chapter documents a shortcrust double-crust apple pie filled with Bramley apple slices, sugar, and a small amount of spice, baked until the pastry is golden and the filling tender. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: the apple pie section documents the same method, noting the importance of using a tart cooking apple that holds a degree of shape under heat. Apple pie using Bramley apples in a double shortcrust case is documented continuously in British domestic cookery from the Victorian period; the recipe here follows the public-domain method.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unbaked assembled pie. Bake from frozen at 180°C fan for 1 hour, covering the top with foil if it colours too fast.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble and refrigerate unbaked for up to a day. The filling can be made 2 days ahead and refrigerated separately.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; partial blind bake at 200°C fan then filled bake at 180°C fan

recipeTools.notes: 23 cm deep pie dish, at least 5 cm deep.

glossaryTerms.definition: A cut made through the top crust of a double-crust pie before baking to allow steam from the filling to escape. Without a vent, steam pressure builds under the lid, causing it to puff, crack, or detach from the sides. For apple pie, cut four 5 cm slits radiating from the centre in a cross, or use a small pastry cutter to cut a decorative hole in the middle.

content.text: Apple pie depends on two things: pastry that stays crisp on the base and a filling that is tender but not watery. Bramley apples are the correct variety — they collapse partially under heat, releasing juice that is absorbed back into the collapsed flesh, producing a filling with body rather than a pool of liquid beneath a soggy base. A partial blind bake on the base before the lid goes on is not optional for a deep-dish pie.

items.prepNote: a little sugar in the pastry adds colour
about 1 kg, peeled, cored, and sliced 5 mm thick
juice only — keeps the apple from browning and sharpens the flavour
thickens the juice to prevent a soggy base
to scatter over the glazed lid

content.text: Combine the {{plain-flour}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{caster-sugar}} in a bowl. Add the {{unsalted-butter}} cold butter and rub in with your fingertips until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs. Add the {{water}} ice cold water gradually, pressing the dough together with each addition, until it just holds when squeezed. Divide into two portions, one slightly larger than the other (base needs more pastry than the lid). Press each into a disc, wrap, and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Roll the larger disc of pastry on a floured surface to 4 mm thick and line a 23 cm deep pie dish, leaving overhang. Prick the base all over with a fork. Line with baking paper, fill with baking beans, and bake for 12 minutes. Remove the beans and paper and bake for a further 5 minutes until the base is dry but not fully golden. This is the insurance against a soggy bottom.

Toss the sliced {{apple-bramley}} Bramley apples with the {{soft-brown-sugar}}, {{cinnamon-ground}}, {{lemon}} lemon juice, and {{cornflour}} until coated. Pile the filling into the partially baked base — it can be heaped generously as the apples collapse during baking. The cornflour will thicken the juice and prevent it pooling at the base.
Reduce the oven to 180°C fan. Roll the smaller disc of pastry to 4 mm and lay it over the filling. Trim and crimp the edges together. Brush the top with {{eggs}} egg wash. Cut

in the top — four 5 cm slits from the centre. Scatter {{caster-sugar}} sugar over if using. Bake for 45–50 minutes until the pastry is deep golden and the filling is tender when tested through the vent with a skewer. Rest for 20 minutes before cutting; the filling firms as it cools.
